item.title: Beijing wants all of society to contribute to the concept of predominance of executive power
item.description:
Ding Xuexiang, Vice Premier of the People's Republic of China, attended a joint meeting with Macau members of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC). At the meeting in Beijing, the official stated that the implementation of the principle of predominance of executive power requires the support and cooperation of all sectors of Macau society.
Beijing wants all of Macau society to contribute to the “effective” implementation of the concept of predominance of executive power in the Macao SAR. The guidance was given by Ding Xuexiang, vice-premier of the People's Republic of China and also responsible for the Central Government for Hong Kong and Macau affairs, in a meeting with members of the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC) of the two special administrative regions. Quoted by Chinese-language Radio Macau, Ding Xuexiang stated at the meeting that “the effective implementation of governance led by the executive branch is not easy” and “requires not only that the Chief Executive and the Government of the Special Administrative Region reinforce their sense of responsibility, but also the cooperation and support of all sectors of society, including the Legislative Assembly and the judiciary”. After the meeting with Ding Xuexiang, some of the CPPCC Macau members expressed support for the Beijing official's words. On Chinese-language Radio Macau, Chui Sai Cheong noted that Ding asked Macau to take advantage of the development opportunities of the 15th Five-Year Plan and play a connector role in international affairs, in addition to the economy. The former deputy to the Legislative Assembly also said that the Chinese vice-premier urged the Macao SAR to maintain national security “with vigilance and awareness” and to develop “patriotic forces in Macau, promoting patriotic education for young people and uniting community organizations”. Lam, who in addition to being a member of the CPPCC is also the Secretary for Social Affairs and Culture of the Macao SAR, highlighted Ding Xuexiang's “important instructions” on how Hong Kong and Macau can integrate and serve national development, focusing mainly on five aspects: “seizing the main opportunities of the 15th Five-Year Plan; supporting the Chief Executive and the Macao SAR Government in strengthening Executive-led development; promoting high-quality development in Hong Kong and Macau; safeguard national security and social stability in Hong Kong and Macau; and develop and strengthen patriotic forces in Hong Kong and Macau.” Wu Zhiliang, member of the CPPCC and chairman of the Board of Directors of the Macau Foundation, said he was “moved” by the words of the Chinese vice-premier, adding that “Macau has always shared the same fate as the country” and that “young people must be offered a platform to understand the latest events in the country, creating opportunities for them to transform from mere spectators into participants and builders”.

item.title: Pensioners must provide proof of life
item.description: Beneficiaries who receive the elderly pension or disability pension must provide proof of life as soon as possible, warned the Social Security Fund (FSS). Benefits for beneficiaries who have not provided proof of life by the end of March will no longer be granted from April onwards. For 2026, around 177 thousand beneficiaries are involved, of which around 14 thousand have not yet provided proof of life. Through the Macau Single Account application, beneficiaries can follow the instructions and provide proof of life through facial recognition. The beneficiary's spouse, parents or children can access the Macau Single Account to help the beneficiary provide proof of life. Beneficiaries can also provide proof of life, with their valid Macau Resident Identity Card, through self-service kiosks installed in various locations in Macau, in the Cooperation Zone in Hengqin and in various cities in the Greater Bay Area, including Guangzhou, Zhuhai, Zhongshan, Jiangmen and Hong Kong, or go in person to designated service points in Macau. As for beneficiaries who cannot go to the service points in person and are unable to provide proof of life via electronic means, they can delegate a representative to deliver the respective supporting documents or send them by post to the FSS. In addition, beneficiaries living in Guangdong province can personally visit social insurance institutions of different hierarchies in Guangdong province, accompanied by their valid ID card, to provide proof of life.

item.title: Nüshu: an intimate and secret language between women that illuminates solidarity
item.description:
This is the only one written exclusively for women in the world. Nüshu, which originated in Hunan province centuries ago as a form of female resistance to educational exclusion, crosses borders and arrived in Macau at the Macau Literary Festival. FINAL POINT, Pan Shengwen, Nüshu activist, considers this language to be a symbol of fraternity and women's empowerment. With characters reminiscent of willow leaves, the writing was a spiritual refuge and is now revitalized by new generations as a timeless emotional connection between women.
The silence imposed on women throughout history has not silenced the female voice in southern China and has given rise to its own language of resistance and solidarity. Nüshu, as the term suggests in Chinese, is “women's writing”, being the only writing system in the world created and used exclusively by women. Circulating discreetly in southern China for centuries, the ancient system faced an extinction crisis, and is attracting more and more women to learn and work to keep this writing alive, resonating the “solidarity and empowerment of women”, said Pan Shengwen in an interview with PONTO FINAL. The activist dedicated to promoting Nüshu, also founder of the “GenZ Nüshu Artistic Development Project”, brought culture to Macau with the workshop “Nüshu. Learning to write the secret language of women”, organized yesterday by the Macau Literary Festival – Rota das Letras. "Nüshu demonstrates female brotherhood. Even though we don't know each other, we feel belonging to this culture, being the only writing system in the world exclusively for women", said Pan, justifying that this is how Nüshu stands out among the different Chinese cultures. As Pan Shengwen explained, Nüshu emerged at a time when women were deprived of education. “They could only learn to write by looking at classes through the window, the light of which destroyed the characters, making Nüshu writing similar to willow leaves”, he explained. Nüshu originated in Jiangyong County, Yongzhou, in the south of Hunan Province, China. Women there used Nüshu to express their emotions and thoughts that were decipherable only by women, thus creating a spiritual refuge inaccessible to men. Even though there are only 396 characters in total, Nüshu users are able to decode texts with context, as one character can have multiple meanings. Pan Shengwen added that women can even create “secret codes” between female friends and family members. The history of Nüshu is centuries old and its exact origin remains unknown. But the earliest documented evidence of this writing appears on a coin issued during the 19th century. Nowadays, few women retain the ability to read and write Nüshu. Its last proficient speaker and writer, Yang Huanyi, died in 2004. And the last natural carrier, that is, the person who used Nüshu in their daily life, He Yanxin, passed away last year. However, since the end of the 20th century, thanks to the efforts of several scholars, this rare female writing has gained international recognition. As a unique and exceptional cultural relic, the Chinese government began to prioritize the preservation of Nüshu, including, since 2006, Nüshu on the List of National Intangible Cultural Heritage of China. With growing awareness about preserving cultural heritage, some young women began to actively learn Nüshu, creating a new generation of heirs. Pan Shengwen is a 21-year-old girl who chose to learn Nüshu on her own initiative, saying that this feminine script gave her strength and a timeless connection of sisterhood. The young woman indicated that her connection with Nüshu began when her mother was diagnosed with cancer, which placed great psychological pressure on the family. "Nüshu gave me immense strength. What impressed me most about her was her profound positivity", he said. "Throughout history, you will discover that many women have had similar experiences to you, albeit in different times and places. That shared feeling allows you to find resonance here," he asserted. Pan Shengwen has therefore committed to Nüshu and hopes to further promote this culture with a view to supporting women in becoming the best version of themselves. “There are many different ways to bloom, but you can still bloom with your own beauty,” he concluded.

item.title: Government promises to implement “two sessions” spirit
item.description:
The secretaries and other holders of the Macao SAR's main positions promised to comply with the guidelines emanating from the so-called “two sessions”, China's main political event. The local government highlighted that this year's “two sessions” have “extremely important significance”.
The plenary session of the National People's Congress (NPC), China's highest legislative body, and the Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC), a consultative body that includes representatives from various sectors of society, are taking place in Beijing. The so-called “two sessions” are considered the main political events in the country. Macau's rulers made a point of expressing support for the guidelines that were emanating from these sessions. In presenting the work report of the Central Government, the Prime Minister of the State Council, Li Qiang, expressed the need to “firmly” implement the principles “one country, two systems”, “Macau governed by its people” and “Macau governed by patriots”, in order to “increase the efficiency of Macau’s governance”, “promote socio-economic development”, support the “integration of Macau into the context of national development and the provision of services to the country”, enhance the “important role of interconnection with the world”, as well as boosting the “prosperity and stability of Macau”. In a statement, Wong Sio Chak, Secretary for Administration and Justice, recalled that this year marks the beginning of the 15th National Five-Year Plan and the 3rd Five-Year Plan of Macau, so the holding of the “two sessions” has a “very important significance”. “The area of ​​Administration and Justice will study and implement, in a comprehensive and in-depth manner, the spirit of the ‘two sessions'”, guaranteed Wong, adding that the decisions and plans of the Central People's Government will be implemented “firmly”, as well as the general perspective of State security and the concept of “predominance of executive power” will be improved, improving “governmental efficiency” and boosting the “progress of the Rule of Law”. Wong Sio Chak also guaranteed that the codes will be reviewed and changed “with the aim of continually raising the level of the Rule of Law in the Macao SAR”, the legal system for defending State security will be “perfected and consolidated” and the reform of Public Administration will be deepened, for example. Lam assured that “the Social Affairs and Culture department will diligently study the spirit of the important instructions given by President Xi during the ‘two sessions’, the Government’s work report, as well as the instructions of Vice Prime Minister Ding”. Furthermore, “it will fully support the Chief Executive and the Macao SAR Government in government action under the primacy of the Law, will persist in the strategic orientation of the predominance of the executive power and will proactively align itself with the 15th National Five-Year Plan”. The Secretary for Social Affairs and Culture promised that her authority will “insist on boosting the integrated development of education, science and technology and qualified personnel”, “continuously improve health protection”, “make the cultural industry flourish and transmit the essential values of love for the country and love for Macau”, “perfect the system of guaranteeing the well-being” of the population and, finally, “encourage the development of the sports sector through the creation of sporting events of renown.” Raymond Tam also stated that his team will “seriously study and implement the spirit of the ‘two sessions’, promoting, on several fronts, the development of urban infrastructures, with a view to linking Macau with the 15th National Five-Year Plan and better integrating into the context of national development and serving the country”. The Secretary for Transport and Public Works assured that his team “will assume important responsibilities in the construction of transport and the optimization of urban infrastructure, areas closely linked to the daily life of the population”. In this sense, “the guidelines for integration in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area will be followed, strengthening the connection between Macau and Hengqin, promoting a liveable and work-friendly urban environment, as well as ecological and low-carbon, safe and efficient development”. The Secretary for Security stated that “it is imperative to seriously grasp and implement the spirit of the ‘two sessions'”. Chan Tsz King promised to lead “the security system forces of the Macao SAR Government in promoting the improvement of the national security system and strengthening national security capabilities in priority areas, intensifying public security control and fully consolidating the security base of the Macao SAR”. “The predominance of executive power guides the decisions of the Central Government, which has the collaboration and support of the Macao SAR Government, the population in general and the various sectors of society”, said the Secretary for Security, adding that the legislative work on the laws and complementary diplomas relating to the Macao SAR State Security Defense Commission are being developed “in a committed manner”, which constitutes “an important step towards responding to and integrating the national development plan”. Finally, the Secretary for Economy and Finance also said that he will “lead” the entities in his area of ​​responsibility in “implementing, in a comprehensive and precise manner, the spirit of the ‘two sessions'”. Tai Kin Kin said that the area of ​​economics and finance “will insist on and improve administrative leadership, constantly strengthening the coordination of policies and execution and implementation capabilities, as well as promoting, in a pragmatic way, the development of adequate diversification of Macau's economy”. Tai Kin Ip highlighted that the “two sessions” have “profound meaning and far-reaching implications, providing fundamental guidance and clear direction for the future development of the Macao SAR”. “All entities in the area of ​​economics and finance will exhaustively study and consciously implement the spirit of the ‘two sessions'”, assured the secretary. In addition to the Government secretaries, also the remaining holders of the main positions of the Macao SAR - such as Song Man Lei, president of the Court of Final Instance, Ao Ieong Seong, Commissioner Against Corruption, André Cheong, president of the Legislative Assembly, Elsie Ao Ieong, Commissioner of Audit, Adriano Marques Ho, director of the Customs Service, Tong Hio Fong, Prosecutor, and Leong Man Cheong, general commander of the Unitary Police Services – made a point of stating that they will take into account the guidelines issued by the “two sessions” when carrying out their respective work.

item.title: Japan deploys first long-range missile developed in the country
item.description: Japan installed the first batch of long-range missiles developed in the country, as part of strengthening its offensive capabilities in the face of increased tensions in the region. The upgraded Type-12 surface-to-sea missiles will be deployed at Kengun Field in southwestern Japan's Kumamoto prefecture by the end of March, completing the deployment process, Japanese Chief Cabinet Secretary Minoru Kihara said. Military vehicles carrying the launchers and other equipment arrived at dawn in a discreet operation, criticized by local residents, who protested near the camp. Opponents accuse the Government of a lack of transparency and consider that the deployment could increase tensions and turn the base into a target for possible attacks. In 2024, the Ministry of Defense brought forward the missile installation schedule by one year. Japan has accelerated military reinforcement in the southwest of the archipelago, at a time when China is increasing pressure on Taiwan. The modernized Type-12 missile, developed by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, has a range of around a thousand kilometers, much higher than the 200 kilometers of the original version, and can reach areas of the Chinese mainland. The system is expected to be installed later at Field Fuji in Shizuoka Prefecture, west of Tokyo, later this year. Japanese Defense Minister Shinjiro Koizumi stated in February that Tokyo also intends to install medium-range air defense systems on the island of Yonaguni, the westernmost island in Japan and located east of Taiwan, by March 2031. Tensions increased in November, after Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, shortly after taking office, stated that possible Chinese military action against Taiwan could justify a Japanese military response. Takaichi has committed to reviewing security and defense policy by the end of the year and intends to strengthen the country's military capabilities with unmanned weapons and long-range missiles. The Japanese Government is also expected to eliminate some restrictions on the export of lethal weapons in the coming weeks, with the aim of boosting the national defense industry and strengthening cooperation with allied countries.

item.title: “The world is on the brink of multiple crises”
item.description:
In the opium trade, in the 19th century, Macau played a central role, as it was the first stop where Chinese smugglers contacted their partners, before heading to Lintin Island. Author of works such as the “Ibis” trilogy, centered on the first Opium War, and works of historical fiction and non-fiction, which explore colonialism, identity and climate change, Indian writer Amitav Ghosh, in the territory to participate in the Macau Literary Festival, in an interview with PONTO FINAL, spoke about past and future works, in addition to the various environmental crises (and beyond) that are currently plaguing the world.
What was Macau's role in this opium trade between China and India? Macau played an essential role. The opium trade was banned in China, so all the people who were selling opium were actually smugglers. The first port they stopped at was Macau. They did not unload opium here, just small quantities, but they made their first stop here and established communications with their Chinese partners, the smuggling networks inside China. Then they would go from here to a place called Lintin Island. During this period, the Qing empire did not exercise direct control over the remote islands. Therefore, he did not have direct control on Lintin Island. The British and Americans turned Lintin Island into a prime spot for opium smuggling. The boats went from here to Lintin Island and then unloaded the opium onto these smuggling ships that they took to the British and American ships that were anchored there. Chinese smugglers delivered the opium and unloaded the ships, so that they did not have any signs in the hold. Then they went north, to the Pearl Delta estuary, where the first Chinese customs station was located. When the inspectors came on board to inspect the boats, they asked: What opium? There is no opium.
Both in fiction and non-fiction, he has also written a lot on subjects such as climate change, sustainability and global warming. When did you start turning to literature to address these topics? I wouldn't say I used literature. I prefer to say that my environmental concerns became part of what I was writing. In the “Ibis” trilogy, a lot is about the environment, when it comes to flowers and the flower trade. Already then, I addressed environmental issues, historically, so that you can understand how environmental and ecological history transformed the planet. The opium trade was part of the process.
How was the opium trade part of the process? It was an extraction process. For example, in the British colonial regime in India, peasants were an important part of the Indo-Gangetic plain. They implemented the opium production regime, which completely transformed the agricultural cycle. They smuggled opium to China, where it was sold to thousands of addicts and all the profits went to them, later taking this capital to London, New York and Boston. They built train lines. One of the main opium traders became rich and his son bought a land as large as Liechtenstein, transforming it completely. The ramifications of the opium trade have become a model: raising capital and creating capital, which generates these models where these massive environmental interventions become possible.
In his work and public interventions, he always makes a connection between colonialism, capitalism and global warming. How are they all related? It has to do exactly with this extraction process and how money is removed from some places. My book, “The Nutmeg Curse”, translated into Portuguese, begins by telling the story of what the Dutch did in Indonesia, on the Moluccas Islands. The Portuguese were the first to arrive at the Moluccas Islands, but there were too few of them to be able to conquer or take them. But the Banda Islands, south of the Moluccas, controlled the entire nutmeg trade, because the nutmeg tree grew only in this region. They had been involved in the nutmeg trade for millennia and this, along with mace, had become valuable as trade goods. The Dutch wanted to take control of the nutmeg trade and arrived in greater numbers than the Portuguese, in addition to being the most violent. When they arrived in the Banda Islands in the 16th century, they tried to impose a treaty on the Bandanes, which would result in the nutmeg trade being completely handed over to the Dutch. Naturally, the Bandanese resisted. But there weren't many, there were only 15,000. In 1621, the governor-general of the Dutch East India Company, Jan Pieterszoon Coen, decided to implement a final solution. He took a fleet to the Banda Islands and, within ten weeks, completely exterminated the population. It killed a third of the population, another third fled to the mountains and died there of hunger and disease. Another third were enslaved. A few hundred Bandanese managed to escape to other islands. They completely changed the environment of the island, because they created these plantations, which they then passed on to Dutch plantation owners. This is the beginning of resource scarcity, a model that also explains what is happening in the Persian Gulf today. There is a direct relationship, because the Portuguese began by setting up their forts in the Strait of Hormuz. They were pioneers in creating this model of controlling chokepoints, which the American empire later inherited. The purpose of this was extraction. Before it was spices, then it was other goods of different types and now it is oil. What we see among the Bandanese is that they had this miraculous tree, the nutmeg tree, which made them rich. They were prosperous people and then it turned into the tree of death. This is what happens with oil. Wherever you see oil, you can also see the difficulties that arise. For a while, it makes people rich, but then it results in incredible violence, because the colonial powers want to control it. We see that this fight continues to happen, in a fundamental way. These struggles are determined by historical factors. By 1510, the Portuguese had established the model for controlling the Indian Ocean. This has been transferred, over the centuries, from one European empire to another. PHOTOGRAPHY GONÇALO LOBO PINHEIRO Considering the state of the world today, does history ever teach anything? The world is on the brink of multiple crises. There are so many crises, which are interconnected, but they are all unfolding at the same time. The world is in a place of so much danger today, which has no historical precedent. We are on the brink of nuclear war. At the same time, the climate crisis is accelerating around us, biodiversity losses are accelerating, species are becoming extinct at the rate of 10 per day, insect populations are falling, our food system has become completely dependent on fossil fuels, sulfur and other chemicals linked to them. The impact of what is happening in the Gulf now will be on food systems. The spring season is approaching and farmers will not have the fertilizers they need to plant. Making our food systems so dependent on fossil fuels has been one of the great catastrophes, because it degrades the earth. I spent a year living and studying in Egypt, in a small village, 50 years ago. Whenever there was a harvest, if the farmers left the land fallow, even for just two or three days, the whole land would turn white, because all the chemicals would come out. It's like heroin addiction: the addict just needs more and more. It's how we have been compensating for the degradation of the quality of the land, by adding more fertilizers and insecticides. It is an unsustainable model and now it will collapse.
What can be the role of literature, also in defending important subjects and themes? Literature used to be at the center of contemporary culture 50/60 years ago. Now it's not so central. Cinema and television took this place, although literature also influenced them. For me, to claim that literature can change or have a massive impact on everything would be to overestimate the potential influence. It would be presumptuous of me to make grandiose claims. At the same time, literature also helps them to become aware of things. I have met hundreds, if not thousands, of young people, in different parts of the world, who tell me that my work has inspired them to do something. It's one of the rewards of being a writer. PHOTOGRAPHY GONÇALO LOBO PINHEIRO Do you have a new project in hand? I have several projects in hand, one of which is the ‘Future Library Project’. And I have other projects at the same time. I have two or three essays that I've been trying to extend into a longer work of nonfiction and these are very much tied to my reading of how the environmental crisis is developing. I'm also working on a novel.
How does the ‘Future Library Project’ work? Two artists — a Scottish woman [Katie Paterson] and a Norwegian woman [Anne Beate Hovind] — created a project, in which a writer contributes a text, which is locked away for 100 years, and will only be released in 2114. None of us will be around to see it. So far, there are 14 more who have done it, like Margaret Atwood and many others. I'm 14th. I can't divulge anything about it.
Do you have any ideas for writing something else about Macau in the future? When I go somewhere, I don't immediately have an idea. Generally, an idea comes to me two or three years later and that's how I put it all together. It's entirely possible that this visit to Macau will plant a seed in my head and that it will lead to something, but at the moment I have no plans for that.

item.title: A map of Macau, through the eyes of Pessanha
item.description:
Where was the poet Camilo Pessanha, who was also a teacher, lawyer and magistrate, when he lived in Macau? In an attempt to recreate the footsteps of the author, who stayed in the territory from 1894 to 1926, Christopher Chu and Maggie Hoi organized a guided tour on the 7th and 8th of March.
It's 11 am, when several people start to arrive, at the agreed meeting point, at Avenida da Praia Grande, number 75. In total, there are almost 40 — Portuguese, Chinese and other nationalities. “This is where Camilo Pessanha died, of tuberculosis, on the 1st of March”, says Christopher Chu, on the 7th of March, pointing to the building where the HSBC bank is now located. “Was it a hospital?” someone asks. “No, no, this is where he lived for the last few years”, he replies. The start of a 1.5 kilometer route was scheduled, which would take that group to emblematic locations linked to the author's passage through the territory. Followed by a large group of people, much larger than what he had expected, Christopher continued the tour, heading to the second point of the visit, while Maggie Choi translated for the Chinese audience. “Before his death, he left everything to his daughter [of the partner he lived with, the concubine Águia de Prata]. He had a terrible relationship with his own son”, comments, out of curiosity, the co-author of the book “Caminho Pessanha’s Macau Stories”. The second point of the visit is the Bank of China building, where the old Hing Kee Hotel was located. Christopher interacts with a group, commenting on some curious facts about Camilo Pessanha's life. “Do you know that this hotel was also known as Fong Hotel, which was the name of a guest?” From there, the visit continued, towards the current Church of Saint Augustine, which was then the Lyceum of Macau. “He met different people here — including the poet Wenceslau de Morais”, he reveals. Christopher continues the walk, now heading towards Rua da Felicidade. “It was probably here where he met Lei Ngoi Long, the concubine he bought from a loan shark. The group continues, enjoying the walk, curious to know the details of Pessanha's life, which Christopher tells, as he progresses, now towards Rua do Tarrafeiro. Halfway along the route, stop at Rua Camilo Pessanha. “It has his name, but this happened, one day after his death”, he says. Arriving at Rua do Tarrafeiro, where the poet bought a book of Chinese poems from the Ming dynasty, which he later translated into “Chinese Elegies”, there is one last stop left. From there we head to Jardim Camões, in an attempt to recreate a photo from the time. “It was a photo of a group of members of the then Macau Institute, Scientific, Literary and Artistic Association,” he says. Among these were figures such as Camilo Pessanha, Bishop D. José da Costa Nunes and Admiral Hugo Lacerda Castelo Branco. “This was an important time for transport and the Macau Industrial Exhibition was about to be organized”, recalls Christopher. The following day, Sunday, the route continued, with four new stops. The group started at Jardim das Artes, where the Statue of Camilo Pessanha is located. We then headed to the Military Club, marked by a period of high political instability in 1912. China underwent a radical political change, putting an end to more than 2,000 years of imperial rule with the fall of the Qing dynasty and the establishment of the Republic of China. Portugal, on the other hand, was facing a monarchical revolt and extremely high international pressure in relation to its colonies. Macau is suddenly between two republics and Camilo Pessanha is in the middle of the crossroads. The visit continued to Jardim Vasco da Gama, where Christopher linked the poet's story with the voyages of navigator Vasco da Gama, which marked the arrival of the Portuguese in Asia. At this second stop, the poem “San Gabriel”, by Pessanha, was recited:
“Saint Gabriel, tutelary archangel, Come again and bless the sea, Come and guide us over the blue plain”
If Vasco da Gama left Lisbon in 1497 on the ship São Gabriel, in search of the sea route to India, Pessanha also made the same crossing centuries later, no longer as a discoverer, but as a poet. And if the navigator had found the spice trade in Calicut, the poet had found in Macau the porcelain, fans and poems from the Ming dynasty that he would translate. In one and another, the same call from the East, the same “final conquest of light, of Good, sweet unreal flash” that Pessanha dreamed of in his verses. Both the epic of the Discoveries and the solitary adventure of Camilo Pessanha shared the same desire, that of being taken, beyond the known horizon, “over the blue plain”. The tour ended at the São Miguel Arcanjo Cemetery, where Camilo Pessanha is buried, next to his son and an unidentified woman who, in life, was responsible for taking care of the poet's tomb, believed to be his granddaughter or daughter-in-law. Over the course of two days, this map drawn by Christopher Chun was, in addition to an exercise in history, a restitution of flesh and blood to a figure who, more than the symbolist poet, revealed himself to be the man, the teacher, the lawyer, the collector of Chinese porcelain, the father.
Guiomar Salema Luciana Leitão

item.title: Humanoid robots worry Macau's community services advisory body
item.description: The frequency of humanoid robots roaming the streets of Macau has been a viral topic on social media after an elderly woman had an interaction with a robot at Fai Chi Kei, an incident that led the resident to call the police. The concern about safety due to humanoid robots was addressed in an intervention before the agenda of the meeting of the North Zone Community Services Advisory Council, last Saturday. Council member Zeng Zengwei mentioned being aware of possible collisions between robots and pedestrians or vehicles, as well as the issue of privacy, as robots can be equipped with cameras and microphones. The member said he noticed more and more humanoid robots circulating on the streets, especially in densely populated areas, such as near schools or bus stops. “During their movements, robots occasionally collide with pedestrians,” pointed out Zeng Zengwei, who said he had witnessed similar cases. The person responsible understands that, as robots are machines and can experience system failures or unforeseen circumstances, collisions between robots and pedestrians or vehicles can give rise to issues related to repairs, medical treatment and even legal liability. Zeng Zengwei, at the same time, suggested that legislation and regulations be created specifically applicable to humanoid robots, which aim to resolve security and order problems arising from their use in public spaces. “Supervision of features such as cameras and microphones that may be covered by the Personal Data Protection Law” must also be carried out, according to the member. Remember that the case of the robot and the elderly woman at Fai Chi Kei took place last Thursday night. In a video shared on the internet, an elderly woman is seen protesting against the robot, and the report in the publication says that the situation required the assistance of an ambulance. Other online videos and images show that some Public Security Police officers arrived at the scene, who later followed the robot's departure from the scene. Several videos and images began to circulate virally on social media platforms, with comments humorously calling it the “first case of arrest of a robot in the world”.

item.title: Casa de Portugal goes to elections on March 18. Amélia António is running again
item.description: The Casa de Portugal in Macau goes to elections on March 18th and the current management bodies are expected to remain in office for another two years. According to a statement shared on the association's social networks, there is only one list for managing bodies, headed by Amélia António, who has presided over the association since 2005. The single list also includes Ricardo Igreja, who is running as vice-president, Diana Soeiro, secretary, and Nuno Figueiredo, treasurer. The list includes Rui Simões, Francisco Ricarte and Pedro D’Alte. The board of the general assembly will be composed of João Manuel Costa Antunes, the president, André Ritchie, vice-president, and Filomena McGuire, secretary. The supervisory board includes Armindo Vaz, Paula Silva and Andrea Aleixo. This year Casa de Portugal in Macau celebrates its 25th anniversary. Created in 2001, the association aims to promote, disseminate and defend the Portuguese language and culture, the heritage and identity of Macau as well as disseminate Chinese culture, through exhibitions, shows, festivals, publications, video productions, and artistic teaching carried out at the School of Arts and Crafts. Casa de Portugal in Macau actively participates in the Chinese New Year Parade, the Macau Arts Festival, the celebrations of the 25th of April, the 10th of June and the 5th of October, in Arraial de S. João, the Cultural Week of Portuguese Speaking Countries, the Lusofonia Festival, the Fringe Festival and the 20th of December Parade, among other events, establishing partnerships with public and private entities.

item.title: Macau chefs discuss the challenges facing local cuisine
item.description: The Macau Literary Festival sessions at Casa Garden opened on Sunday afternoon with a discussion on the future of Macanese cuisine, featuring four of the region's most prominent chefs and the launch of the cookbook “Recipes with a Hope”. Chefs André Lai, Daniel Lai, Florita Alves and Sónia Palmer will be present at the session. André Lai, executive chef at Andaz Macau, described the recipe book as an essential historical record: “The book serves to leave a record in Macau of the chefs who are currently here, there are not many so it is an extremely important mission”, he said, adding that chefs must act as “ambassadors of culture”. During the panel, the four chefs reached a consensus that Macanese cuisine is at a crossroads. Sónia Palmer, with more than 30 years in the sector, expressed concern about its longevity and lamented that hotels “aren't making an effort to promote Macanese food”, also pointing to a disconnect with young people: “Unfortunately, the younger generation doesn't know how to appreciate Macanese food, but it's not their fault either”. Florita Alves, chef at La Famiglia, agreed with this opinion, suggesting that the problem lies in presentation and education: “The new generation doesn't want to know what homemade food is like because the presentation is not inviting,” noted Alves. “Translating culture into gastronomy is an education that has to be done at home”. André Lai also offered his perspective on modernization versus tradition. He criticized the lack of passion in the industry, arguing that many restaurants focus exclusively on business, instead of transmitting the love for cuisine and tradition, and left an appeal to the government to support and recognize professionals who are, in fact, dedicated. Despite the obstacles, the four chefs agreed that “Recipes with a Hope” is a step in the right direction, an accessible book that brings the flavors of Macau from their professional kitchens to the homes of a new generation.

item.title: Macau tourism, culture and art highlighted in London
item.description: A Macau delegation made up of representatives from the tourism industry and local cultural and artistic groups organized a promotion seminar in London last Friday to expand the international visitor market. The event coordinated by the Directorate of Tourism Services (DST) had the support of the National Tourism Office of China in London and aimed to disseminate the most recent tourist information on the territory to tour operators and visitors from the United Kingdom. The director of the art and culture group “MyLand Culture”, Pal Lok Chok, was also invited to the event to present Macau's cultural heritage and the artistic projects being developed in the region. In turn, Filipe Dores, artist and representative of the restaurant industry, shared impressions related to the transformation of everyday tea culture into a “cultural practice of contemporary art”, as stated in a statement from DST. The seminar also included the tasting of a special cocktail, entitled “MACAO”, the offer of special souvenirs, the exhibition of limited edition works and the exhibition of the painting “Timeless Voyage”, inspired by Macau's role in the Maritime Silk Road. The event was attended by more than a hundred representatives from the tourist industry from Macau and the United Kingdom, including the director of DST, Helena de Senna Fernandes. In her speech, the official mentioned that the visa-free policy for visitors from the United Kingdom when entering China, in force since February, “created more convenient conditions” for them to explore Macau and the Greater Bay Area. According to the DST statement, Helena de Senna Fernandes said she hoped that the event in London would help to showcase Macau's dynamism to the British market and contribute to strengthening cooperation with UK tour operators. The director of the Chinese National Tourism Office in London, Zhang Li, also spoke at the event, noting that the city of Macau is “intimately linked to the socio-economic development of the Motherland” and guaranteeing that the institution he represents will take responsibility for promoting Macau in the United Kingdom and building a more fluid exchange bridge between the two parties.

item.title: “I love Macau, but I don’t know it”
item.description: They are small chronicles, published over the years, linked to transport in Lisbon and Macau. Yesterday was the launch of “Crónicas da Carris e da Mesquita ao Nam Van”, by Ana Paula Barros, presented by Vera Borges, professor at the City University of Macau. The room was too small for the flood of friends, colleagues and students who came. The book “Crónicas da Carris e da Mesquita ao Nam Van”, by Ana Paula Barros, a writer based in the territory, launched yesterday, at the Macau Literary Festival, contains stories, which result from the author's observation, in the use of means of transport, in Lisbon and Macau. Using a language that tends to be colloquial, these chronicles “talk about her and the people she came across”, says Vera Borges, professor at the City University of Macau, in charge of the presentation. They are chronicles of the moment and, in some cases, historical — for example, there is mention of the funeral of the former President of the Republic of Portugal, Mário Soares. The first part of the book takes place in Lisbon and is a “very particular perspective” of trips made on Carris or the city’s metro. There is a “wandering”, with “implicit dialogues between the viewer and the objects”, also realizing that she is an author of causes. “There is a desire to contribute to changing reality”, says Vera Borges. There is a portrait of everyday life, seeking to record “normality”, also revealing the author’s imprisonment in “a violent reality”. “Women who seek to express themselves as subjects, almost transgressive”, he declares. It is also noted that the texts are “glued to the author’s life”, with all the good and bad that this may involve. The book grows until it ends in Macau, with the author's reports, using Transmac. “Macau is a time of reconciliation and here the texts are more like short essays”, says the teacher. Chronicles shared on Facebook After the presentation, the author, visibly moved by the community's turnout and the presenter's words, stated that these chronicles were published over seven years on Facebook. Seeking to contextualize, the author states that, in 2017, when she arrived in Macau, she was going through a “very complicated particular situation”, but she immediately felt better, given the city's atmosphere. “It was relatively familiar and provincial”, just like Viseu, the city of origin. Not wanting to reveal too much of the book's content, about Macau, when questioned by the audience, she resorts to a metaphor. “What impressed me most about Macau were the smells,” he says. "I've been here for seven and a half years. I love Macau, but I don't know it", he explains.

item.title: The heart of Macau, reflected in the works of Gao Lipeng
item.description:
The “Mirrored Heart” exhibition will open today at 6:30 pm at Fundação Rui Cunha, where it will remain open to the public until March 21. In total, 26 paintings will be on display depicting the streets, light and history of Macau, reinterpreted through the pastel perspective of artist Gao Lipeng.
Today, the Rui Cunha Foundation will open the exhibition “Coração Espelhado”, a collection of 26 works by painter Gao Lipeng. The exhibition opens to the public at 6:30 pm, remaining on display until March 21st. Entry is free. From an initial set of more than three hundred pieces, curators Jun Zilan and Lalin, under the academic guidance of Wang Lan, highlighted 26 paintings inspired by the artist's walks around the city of Macau over the last three years. In a statement, the Rui Cunha Foundation says that “the set reveals a choice of the artist's most representative works”, drawn in Chinese ink and characterized by pastel tones, “which demonstrate his spiritual reconstruction of the history and cultural landscape of Macau”. As Macau is a city where different cultures dialogue and extremes touch, Gao Lipeng's works seek to reflect – or mirror – these same “historical encounters between Eastern and Western cultures”, through the “architectural texture” and the “layers of light and shadow” that paint the city. Advisor Wang Lan, quoted in the Rui Cunha Foundation statement, explains that “this exhibition summarizes Gao Lipeng’s academic phase in Macau and interprets his quest to ‘see the heart through art’ – the concept of ‘mirror heart’”. Chinese ink is used to “address contemporary themes of urban landscapes, paving a visual path for viewers that connects personal experiences with collective memory”. In the exhibition manifesto, the painter's artistic expression is characterized as a fusion of tradition with modernity and academic rigor with creative innovation, which gives him a unique place in contemporary Chinese painting. “Gao Lipeng’s art integrates meticulous traditional Chinese brushwork with contemporary artistic perspectives, reflecting his deep involvement with cultural heritage and urban narratives, particularly through his thematic works on Macau”, it reads. Gao Lipeng studied Fine Arts at the Macau University of Science and Technology (MUST) and is currently an active painter in the city's artistic scene. In parallel, he holds positions such as member of the China Artists Association (CAA), visiting researcher at Peking University, board member of the Peking University Alumni Painting Calligraphy Association, board member of the Shadong Young Artists Association, painter at the Young Artists Academy of the Li Keran Painting Academy and researcher at the Dunhuang Chinese Painting Research Institute.

item.title: Government wants to ban possession and consumption of electronic cigarettes
item.description:
The Macau authorities presented a proposed law that provides for a ban on the possession and consumption of electronic cigarettes, “in view of the risks associated with public health”.
The Macau Health Services announced the launch of a public consultation, between March 8th and April 8th, on the revision of the smoking prevention and control law. Since 2018, the sale, advertising and promotion of electronic cigarettes has been prohibited in Macau. In 2022, the Chinese region also banned the manufacture, distribution, import, export and transportation of these devices. But the head of the Office for the Prevention and Control of Smoking and Alcoholism at the Health Services admitted that residents have continued to use electronic cigarettes, which “due to their small size are easily concealable”. "Electronic cigarettes are not better than traditional cigarettes. But, with different flavors and different shapes, they are more attractive and can attract consumption among young people", lamented Lam Chong. “Some people, after some time, start smoking traditional cigarettes”, he added, at a press conference. Furthermore, the leader warned that electronic cigarettes have been used “as a tool” for the consumption of illegal drugs, such as the case of a new synthetic drug, known as ‘space oil’. “The population, in general, demands the strengthening of supervision over the consumption of electronic cigarettes”, defended the Health Services, in the public consultation document. 'Space oil', produced from the anesthetic etomidate, has already caused at least three deaths in the neighboring region of Hong Kong. In Macau, the first seizure was made at a local school in October 2023. The substance is known in Hong Kong as a 'zombie drug' because it can cause serious physical and mental harm, including addiction, memory loss, seizures, loss of consciousness and even death. The Health Services highlighted that the possession of electronic cigarettes has been banned in Singapore since 2018 and that Hong Kong will ban consumption in public places from 30 April. “We hope the review can come into force next year, although there will be a transition period,” said Lam Chong. After entry into force, anyone caught in public with electronic cigarettes will face a fine of up to 1,500 patacas. The proposed law also provides for a ban on the manufacture, distribution, import, export and transportation into and out of Macau of nicotine pouches, herbal cigarettes and tobacco or water pipe paste. The objective, explained Lam Chong, is to ban these alternative tobacco products, which are not covered by current legislation, “before they become widespread” in the territory, particularly among young consumers. The leader warned that manufacturers have wrongly described these products as “tobacco-free”, “harmless” or as “healthy substitutes” for conventional cigarettes. Lam gave as an example the consumption of a hookah for 45 to 60 minutes, something he said was “equivalent to smoking 100 cigarettes”, with “a greater amount of carbon monoxide”. Lusa

item.title: MP wants Macau as legal interlocutor between China and Portuguese-speaking countries
item.description:
The Attorney General of Macau said that the Public Ministry will play the role of judicial interlocutor between China and Portuguese-speaking countries. During the Two Sessions, the Central Government once again highlighted Macau's strategic role in connecting with Portuguese-speaking countries.
The Attorney General of Macau, Tong Hio Fong, pointed out that the city's Public Prosecutor's Office will play the role of jurisdictional interlocutor between China and Portuguese-speaking countries. During the Two Sessions taking place in Beijing, the Central Government of China once again highlighted Macau's strategic role in connecting with Portuguese-speaking countries. The work report presented by Prime Minister Li Qiang highlighted that the region must enhance its unique advantages and deepen its role as an interconnection platform between the interior of China and the Portuguese-speaking space, in order to promote “long-term prosperity and stability”. As the Public Ministry has now highlighted, in a statement reacting to the Two Sessions, the Macau Special Administrative Region will be consolidated as a platform for judicial cooperation and professional communication, in order to bring legal systems closer together and promote exchanges between magistrates and institutions from Portuguese-speaking countries. The same organization intends to deepen collaboration in the Guangdong–Hong Kong–Macau Greater Bay Area and in the Hengqin Deep Cooperation Zone, but always with the perspective that Macau should serve as a “bridgehead” for China's opening to the Portuguese-speaking world. According to Tong, the objective is for Macau to increasingly assert itself as a “privileged link between China and the Portuguese-speaking international community”, contributing to national development and consolidating its strategic position within the ‘One Country, Two Systems’ policy. Macau's government plans for 2026 include the signing of an agreement for mutual legal assistance in criminal matters with Angola. Angola and mainland China signed an extradition treaty in 2006, which was only approved by the Angolan Parliament in 2011, and was ratified by the Government of Luanda two years later. According to the Government Action Lines for next year, Macau also wants to start negotiating an “agreement on the reciprocal confirmation and execution of judicial decisions in civil and commercial matters” with Portugal. However, the document does not mention any date for the conclusion of negotiations and the eventual signing of an agreement after all. Macau and Portugal signed an agreement in 2019 regarding the surrender of fugitive offenders, whose criminal legality was called into question by the Portuguese Bar Association. The protocol is not in force, as it was not voted on in the Assembly of the Republic. Lusa

item.title: Lawrence Osborne returns to Macau to tell the story of the peninsula's most daring literary adaptation
item.description: The velvet coat arrived first. Then came the character. Then came the romance. And finally, more than two decades after Lawrence Osborne first entered the Grand Lisboa, came the film, shown just a few blocks away from where he strutted around in borrowed clothes, pretending to be a lord. At the 15th edition of the Macau Literary Festival, author Lawrence Osborne and producer Mike Goodridge met again on the panel “High Wager in Macau: Adapting the Ballad of a Minor Player from Paper to Screen”, which offered a special insight into the creative alchemy that turns a novel into a film. For Osborne, the book was not a product of research, but of immersion: “I never go to places to do research, I don't believe in that at all”, he confessed. His connection to Macau began in the early 2000s, with stays at the Grand Lisboa that lasted from three days to three weeks. "I realized that the hotel had a casino where I could participate, day and night, twenty-four hours a day. For me, this was something extremely new and, in a way, attractive." Osborne became fascinated with the “psychosis of gambling” and what he called the “supernatural element of gambling in China,” which evolved into a desire to write. The main character, Brendan Reilly, the fraudulent “Lord Doyle” at the center of the story, was born from Osborne's own observations: “I had a velvet coat made at the tailors on the ground floor of the Grand Lisboa and when I wore it in the elevator I realized that everyone was looking at me... and I liked it”, admitted the author. “He [Lord Doyle] is a guy who is a failure, a fraud… but he can wear a velvet coat and everyone will look at him, so he might as well call himself a lord and see where that gets him.” The journey from page to screen began when producer Mike Goodridge read the book at Osborne's suggestion in 2017. Although he immediately doubted whether it would be possible to make a film in Macau, the producer didn't give up on the idea. What emerged later, also the result of the work of director Edward Berger and screenwriter Rowan Joffé, was a transformation of Osborne's quiet and introspective novel into what the director of photography, James Friend, described as a “caricatural fever dream”. The visual language was designed to mirror the “superficial and bombastic” environments, according to Friend, from Macau, with the use of custom anamorphic lenses and a 17 millimeter fisheye lens to create the oppressive and suffocating spaces present throughout the plot. The adaptation also made significant changes to create a traditional three-act structure. Rowan Joffé admitted that although he wanted to preserve the “fantasy and poetry” of the book, he felt it lacked a clear plot for a mainstream audience, and so a “ticking time bomb” was introduced in which Lord Doyle has three days to pay his debts to the hotel. As for the characters, an entirely new one was added, Cynthia Blithe, played by Tilda Swinton, the working-class antagonist who personifies the consequences of Doyle's fraud. The character Dao-Ming was also reimagined, from a prostitute in the novel to an illicit gambling loan agent in the film, played by Fala Chen. Throughout the panel, both Lawrence Osborne and Mike Goodridge emphasized the need for a creative separation. Regarding the theme, Osborne argued that "a lot of writers are very bitter about what they see on the screen because it doesn't agree with what they wrote, but that's nonsense. They can't be the same, they shouldn't be the same", describing some of his own experience: "When I first saw the film, I was a little surprised... It's someone else's vision, but it has to be someone else's vision and I'm glad it was, it gives a different life to the same story." After the session at Casa Garden, participants headed to the historic Cinema Alegria, one of Macau's remaining independent cinemas, for a special screening of the film “Balada de Um Gambler”.

item.title: Prices rise in China at the highest rate in three years
item.description:
The consumer price index in China increased by 1.3% in February compared to the same month in 2025, the Chinese National Bureau of Statistics reported yesterday.
This is the biggest increase since January 2023 (2.1%), in a month in which the Lunar New Year holidays took place this year, which the authorities were counting on to stimulate consumption. For some years now, China has been subject to deflationary pressures caused by weak domestic demand, production surpluses, a serious housing crisis and high unemployment among young people. Producers wage an aggressive price war to encourage purchases and reduce stock surpluses. The 1.3% increase is higher than the forecasts of analysts consulted by the financial news agency Bloomberg, which pointed to 0.9%. It is also the fifth consecutive month of increase in the index. Zichuan Huang, an economist at Capital Economics, put the rise in prices in perspective, attributing it to “temporary factors, such as the easing of oil deflation and the volatility of food and tourism prices around the New Year”, he wrote in a note. “Tensions in the Middle East will continue to fuel inflation as long as global energy prices remain high,” he says. However, the disappointing results, according to Zichuan, of the political event known as Two Sessions, currently taking place in Beijing, with regard to domestic demand, “should halt any acceleration in inflation as soon as tensions calm down”, he warned. The Chinese economy is seeking to regain the dynamism it had before the start of the Covid-19 pandemic in 2019-2020. Despite the vitality of exports and a record trade surplus of almost 1.2 trillion dollars in 2025, it faces serious structural imbalances and trade pressures from the United States. The Chinese government announced last week that it intends to achieve growth between 4.5% and 5% in 2026, the most modest target set since 1991. Stimulating domestic demand and reducing dependence on exports is one of the challenges announced by the Government and mentioned in the 2026-2030 Five-Year Plan, currently under analysis by thousands of regime representatives gathered in Beijing for the “Two Sessions”. On that occasion, the Government established an inflation target of 2% in 2026. In recent months, authorities have taken steps to stimulate household spending, including a subsidy program for electronics, household appliances and furniture, and were counting on the boosting effect of the Lunar New Year, a period when Chinese people consume and travel more. The holidays reached an exceptional official duration of nine days. Authorities had previously announced new measures to encourage families to spend, for example in the form of shopping vouchers. Lusa

item.title: Five students infected in collective case of gastroenteritis
item.description: The Health Services detected a case of collective gastroenteritis infection at Escola São Paulo, located in Rampa dos Cavaleiros, with five students being infected. Patients began to show symptoms from last Thursday, for example vomiting and abdominal pain, with the majority undergoing treatment in health institutions, according to the Health Services, adding that there were no reports of serious cases or other serious complications. The possibility of alimentary gastroenteritis was excluded according to patients' meal times. According to the times of occurrence of the disease, the symptoms, the incubation period, it is likely that the pathogen is related to a viral infection. The Health Services explain that norovirus and rotavirus are common viral gastroenteritis diseases, spreading very easily. Norovirus infection is easy to occur in public facilities, particularly in nursing homes and schools, as well as among various age groups. The transmission route includes the consumption of food or water possibly contaminated by this virus; contact with vomit or waste from sick people; contact with contaminated objects; or transmission by droplets of saliva. The incubation of the infection normally occurs between 24 and 48 hours. It should be noted that the symptoms of both contagious diseases are identical, namely nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain and mild fever. In general, the symptoms are mild, and these are self-limiting illnesses, lasting one to five days, without complications. The Health Services recommend that the population pay attention to personal, environmental and food hygiene.

item.title: Five decades of activity: The work of Carlos Marreiros in retrospect
item.description:
When architect Carlos Marreiros set up his first exhibition in Macau, in 1976, he was a 19-year-old artist who did things that the conservative territory “didn’t expect”. Fifty years later, a full house at Fundação Oriente gathered to examine a career that spanned architecture, visual arts and cultural leadership, tracing the arc of a professional who, as one guest noted, helped shape Macau's very identity.
The Macau Literary Festival – Rota das Letras organized, as part of its 15th edition, a panel dedicated to the career of the Macanese architect and artist Carlos Marreiros. The session held on Saturday, entitled “Half a Century of Light and Memory: The Artistic Journey of Carlos Marreiros”, featured guests Ung Vai Meng, Carlos Morais José and Roberto Francieri and the artist and curator Alice Kok as moderator. Carlos Marreiros opened his segment on the panel with a historical correction that challenged conventional narratives about the development of his homeland: "People think that the boom in Macau only happened with casinos, but no. The boom in Macau started in the 70s", reiterated the architect. "Young builders, promoters and intellectuals began to develop Macau. On the one hand it was very good, on the other hand it was bad, because it was the beginning of the destruction of heritage, which continued in the 80s and 90s." This observation places Marreiros’ emergence as an artist in a very particular historical moment. Born in Macau on January 1, 1957, the architect came of age in a territory where traditional industries still operated. The 1970s witnessed the last decades of the Macau match industry, which produced matches recognized as the best in the Far East, and the panchão industry, which would only disappear completely at the end of the 1990s. These were the economic foundations on which the coming transformations would be built. Cartographic records from the mid-1970s show a peninsula that gained land from the sea through continuous reclamation over more than a century, with former coastal areas transformed into urban areas. The Macau of Marreiros' youth was already changing, although the direction of that transition continued to be contested. During the panel, Carlos Marreiros described his first exhibition in 1976 and offered an insight into the cultural climate of the time: "I grew up here and, in 1976, precisely 50 years ago, I had my first exhibition in Macau. I did things that Macau did not expect", he recalled. "At that time, there were very good artists, but they usually emigrated because they were too ambitious to survive here. It was a very difficult time, because it was very conservative." This conservatism reflected Macau's position as a cultural crossroads that had not yet fully recognized its own hybridization. The unique character of the territory, which Marreiros summarized in the neologism “Mediterrasia”, (a combination of the words “Mediterranean” and “Asia”) remained latent, waiting to be articulated by artists willing to remain. Addressing the younger generations directly, Carlos Marreiros expressed that “young people should be very grateful to those men of the 70s, for the effort they made to develop Macau, in various ways”. However, he also claimed his place in this story: “I am very happy with my role in the process of this development”. The panel dedicated to the architect's 50-year career did not mark a culmination, but rather a milestone. Carlos Marreiros maintains his practice, his teaching positions and his association leadership. His work continues to be presented in group and individual exhibitions internationally.

item.title: “I have always been fascinated by North Korea”
item.description:
Known for his autobiographical travel diaries, in graphic novel format, Guy Delisle, author of books such as “Shenzhen” and “Pyongyang”, reveals that, among all the trips he made, perhaps North Korea was the most impactful destination as it was, in 2001, “almost forgotten”.
When he arrived in Pyongyang, he was asked to pick up a flower and place it at the foot of the statue of Kim Jong-il, the country's leader in 2001. He immediately realized that the potential for a great story was there. “I have always been fascinated by North Korea”, he revealed, in the session that took place at Casa Garden, on March 6th, as part of the Macau Literary Festival. Born in Canada, Guy Delisle worked in different animation studios, traveling to countries such as France, Germany, China and North Korea. It was after his experiences in Asia that he began writing and illustrating travel diaries, then dedicating himself more to comics. Journalist Sara Figueiredo Costa, who was moderating the session, asked if there were points of contact between the two genders. In response, the author rejects this possibility, arguing that animation ends up being more tied to the television format and the children's audience, while “comics are much more open and free”. Are the four travel diaries published, “Shenzhen”, “Pyongyang”, “Chronicles of Burma” and “Chronicles of Jerusalem”, actually postcards for the family? Often mistaken for a journalist, as he spends most of these trips taking notes, Guy Delisle admits that these books have the feeling of being almost postcards for the family.
A factory, a kidnapping and parenthood
In “Summers at the Factory”, Guy Delisle talks about the “hard” period in which he worked in the factory where his father worked, while in “Hostage” the author portrays the kidnapping of Christophe André, a humanitarian worker, in the Caucasus region, in 1997. “He was kidnapped for three months and, due to a mistake on the part of his kidnappers, he managed to escape”, he says. Inspired by the story, he states that “it was difficult”, but “challenging”, creating a graphic novel in which he describes what a person felt during the 300 days he was trapped in a room, ending up escaping, after his kidnappers forgot to handcuff him, leaving the door open. "What would I do, if I were in that situation? Would I open the door?" On the other hand, in the series of comics he published about parenting, Guy Delisle takes his own experience and illustrates it with humor. Regarding whether his visit to Macau could be the source of a new travel diary, Guy Delisle says it is difficult, as he has little time in the territory. “I would need to stay here much longer”, he says, guaranteeing that, only after spending three to six months in a place, he feels capable of writing about it.
